topic.what: AGA review evaluating the evidence for long-term PPI risks including kidney disease, C. difficile infection, fractures, dementia, and other reported associations
topic.how: Expert-opinion review systematically appraising observational studies and meta-analyses on PPI-associated adverse outcomes, grading evidence quality and distinguishing association from causation
topic.why_matters: Provides an evidence-based counterbalance to alarm about PPI risks, demonstrating that most reported associations are based on low-quality evidence with significant confounding, while supporting appropriate deprescribing when PPIs lack clear indication

characteristics.what_it_measures: Strength and consistency of evidence linking long-term PPI use to reported adverse outcomes
characteristics.value: most associations supported only by low-quality observational evidence with residual confounding
characteristics.vs_baseline: effect sizes generally small (OR 1.1-1.7) and inconsistent across studies
characteristics.evidence: systematic appraisal of observational studies and meta-analyses
characteristics.context: Population-level observational data; no RCTs designed to assess long-term PPI harms

not_for: specific drug comparisons within PPI class

novelty: Systematic demonstration that most PPI-harm associations rest on low-quality evidence with significant confounding
Framework for rational PPI deprescribing based on indication strength rather than blanket risk avoidance
open_problems: RCTs specifically designed to assess long-term PPI safety are ethically and practically challenging
Causal mechanisms for reported associations (kidney, bone, infection) remain unproven
Optimal deprescribing protocols and patient selection criteria need prospective validation
